5	  Scenario 58 pinned a "SessionStart" hook in ".claude/settings.json" carrying the inotifywait pipeline. Test-harness BC empirically demonstrated a hook-model mismatch: Claude Code awaits SessionStart hooks synchronously, so a foreground "inotifywait -m" never returns and session startup hangs / times out. The schema and the host prereqs are fine; the structural mismatch is that synchronous hook delivery is incompatible with a long-running watcher process. Brief 003 (lead-0h1) commits the invariant that every shop is reactive on session start — the realization revision (lead-o00) shifts the mechanism: instead of the SessionStart hook running the watcher, the canonical "CLAUDE.md" primer instructs the router (Claude session) to arm the in-session "Monitor" tool on the validated pipeline at session start. This scenario carries the new realization-pin: the activation instruction is package-data property of the canonical "CLAUDE.md" primer.
6	
7	  The Implementer should retire scenario 58's content from repos/shopsystem-templates/features/brief_003_event_driven_activation_and_settings_json_bootstrap.feature (BC-side hash 679d227f04533ad4) when landing this scenario.
8	
9	  Scenario Outline: the canonical "CLAUDE.md" primer template for each shop type contains an instruction telling the session router to arm the in-session "Monitor" tool, at session start, on a line-buffered "inotifywait" pipeline watching the shop-type's inbound mailbox surface, so the in-session Monitor primitive — not a synchronous SessionStart hook — carries the reactivity invariant
10	    When I ask the "shop-templates" package for the canonical "CLAUDE.md" primer template for shop type "<shop_type>" through its public template-access surface

12	    And the returned body contains the literal substring "Monitor"
13	    And the returned body contains the literal substring "session start"
14	    And the returned body contains the literal substring "stdbuf -oL inotifywait"
15	    And the returned body contains the literal substring "-m -e create,moved_to"
16	    And the returned body contains the literal substring "<watch_target>"
17	    And the returned body does not contain any instruction to arm the watcher via a "SessionStart" hook in ".claude/settings.json"

Under Claude Code's `@`-import resolution, the resolved `CLAUDE.md` content the assistant sees contains the imported text. Today's scenarios already pin "the canonical primer body contains <substring>"; under F that body lives in `.claude/canonical/<shop-type>-primer.md` which is what `update` writes — the scenario as written *still passes* against the canonical asset (it's just a different filename in package data). For 45, the substring check needs adjustment because the bootstrap-written file is now the minimal `CLAUDE.md` *plus* `.claude/shop/name.md` — but the property "after bootstrap, the resolved agent context names the shop's identity" holds; phrasing A preserves it. **My call: Phrasing A** — but I will amend scenarios 45 and 71/72/73 with a supersede-style scenario only if the literal substring tests would now fail against the renamed/relocated canonical asset. Inspecting more closely: scenario 71's test is *"ask the shop-templates package for the canonical CLAUDE.md primer template for shop type X"*. Under F there are now TWO canonical assets per shop type: the `CLAUDE.md` body (imports only) and the `<shop-type>-primer.md` (substantive primer text including the Monitor directive). The "canonical CLAUDE.md primer template" public surface continues to exist but its content shifts. Phrasing A requires that the substring assertions resolve against whichever asset carries the Monitor text — i.e., the canonical primer file under F's new model. Rather than amending 71/72/73 to name the new asset path, I'll let the Implementer interpret "the canonical primer template" as the asset that carries primer content under whichever model is in force. **Decision: Phrasing A on 45/71/72/73, with no scenario amendments needed** — they continue to express the agent-observable property; the public template-access surface remains "the canonical primer template for shop type X" and points at whatever package-data asset carries primer text. That's `assign_scenarios` for the 15. Scenario 39 is pinned today; the F contract contradicts it — that's `request_bugfix` (or a supersede scenario in the new assign). **Final decision: bundle all 16 into one `assign_scenarios`.** Reason: scenario 39's amendment is *expressed* as a supersede preamble (same pattern as scenarios 71/72/76 already use against earlier scenarios). The `lead-eyx` dispatch's preamble claim was wrong. I asserted scenario 39's intent had no BC-side pinned mirror; the BC produced three concrete file+line citations contradicting that. The earlier "byte-for-byte" grep I used was too narrow — the BC-side scenarios use plain phrasing ("does not overwrite or otherwise modify"). - **`a15dac2f87549b8a` — RETIRE.** Intent equivalence verified mechanically. Identity-naming-via-splice is replaced by the trio `4926d80eff389e4f` (name.md written) + `b5994058515b07eb` (CLAUDE.md body has `@.claude/shop/name.md` import) + `83e120795fec908a` (resolution surfaces it). Role-set-naming is replaced by `a6d87c442aa93b5a` (per-shop-type primer file) + `83e120795fec908a`. PO sub-dispatch not required.
- **`0cce58eb573d3c91` — RETIRE.** Single template surface explicitly split into two by `b5994058515b07eb` (body) and `a6d87c442aa93b5a` (primer).

## Mechanism observation

`shop-msg` has no lead-side clarify-reply subcommand. `shop-msg respond` is BC-only (clarify/work_done/mechanism_observation); `shop-msg send` offers only `assign_scenarios | request_bugfix | request_maintenance`. # Report

## Empirical pre-state re-verification

Read `repos/shopsystem-templates/features/bootstrap_cli_surface.feature` directly at the three named lines:

- **Line 152** (`@scenario_hash:56a0ac7107ba5c15`): "update does not overwrite or otherwise modify the target directory's top-level 'CLAUDE.md', even when the canonical 'CLAUDE.md' primer template has changed" — present, exactly as the BC described. The Then-step asserts byte-for-byte equality before vs. after `update`.
- **Line 200** (`@scenario_hash:0cce58eb573d3c91`): "shopsystem-templates ships a canonical 'CLAUDE.md' primer template for each shop type, accessible through the same package-data surface" — present. Asserts the returned body "is the source of truth from which the bootstrap entry point generates the target directory's top-level CLAUDE.md".
- **Line 212** (`@scenario_hash:a15dac2f87549b8a`): "the top-level 'CLAUDE.md' that bootstrap writes... names the shop's own identity — its shop name and its role set" — present. Asserts CLAUDE.md content contains the literal `<shop_name>` substring and names the canonical role set.

## Honest acknowledgment of prior factual error

The `lead-eyx` dispatch's preamble claim was wrong. I asserted scenario 39's intent had no BC-side pinned mirror; the BC produced three concrete file+line citations contradicting that. The earlier "byte-for-byte" grep I used was too narrow — the BC-side scenarios use plain phrasing ("does not overwrite or otherwise modify"). I should have inspected `bootstrap_cli_surface.feature` directly given that it is the file alt F most directly affects.

## Per-conflict call

- **`56a0ac7107ba5c15` — RETIRE.** Directly inverted by new scenario `399908ab1dead40a` (update overwrites CLAUDE.md from canonical). The preservation contract relocates to `.claude/shop/primer.md`. (Flagged in the message: the explicit non-touch pin for `.claude/shop/primer.md` is not in `lead-eyx`; BC may clarify on `lead-mnx` if they want it added.)
- **`a15dac2f87549b8a` — RETIRE.** Intent equivalence verified mechanically. Identity-naming-via-splice is replaced by the trio `4926d80eff389e4f` (name.md written) + `b5994058515b07eb` (CLAUDE.md body has `@.claude/shop/name.md` import) + `83e120795fec908a` (resolution surfaces it). Role-set-naming is replaced by `a6d87c442aa93b5a` (per-shop-type primer file) + `83e120795fec908a`. PO sub-dispatch not required.
- **`0cce58eb573d3c91` — RETIRE.** Single template surface explicitly split into two by `b5994058515b07eb` (body) and `a6d87c442aa93b5a` (primer).

## Response sent

- Vehicle: `request_bugfix` (no scenarios; description-only authorization).
- Reason: lead-side `shop-msg` has no clarify-reply subcommand; same-`work_id` resend would clobber the canonical inbox YAML for `lead-eyx`. Fresh work_id `lead-mnx` (P1, in_progress) preserves `lead-eyx` and continues the conversation additively.
- File deposited: `/workspaces/shopsystem-product/repos/shopsystem-templates/inbox/lead-mnx.yaml` (3273 bytes; verified via `shop-msg pending inbox`).
- Scope marker in the message: additive; new scenarios in `lead-eyx` remain canonical; the retirements fold into the `lead-eyx` work product; `work_done` signs off under `lead-eyx`.

## Mechanism observation

`shop-msg` has no lead-side clarify-reply subcommand. `shop-msg respond` is BC-only (clarify/work_done/mechanism_observation); `shop-msg send` offers only `assign_scenarios | request_bugfix | request_maintenance`. The lead-side workaround — fresh-work_id `request_bugfix` with description-only — works but conflates "answer your clarify" with "I have new bug-fix work for you," and forces a new beads ID for what is conceptually one conversation. This is shopsystem-messaging BC surface, not templates. Worth surfacing as a `mechanism_observation`-shaped finding against shopsystem-messaging when a related work item is in flight there. Not filed here; flagged for the router.

## Beads

`lead-eyx` remains in_progress (BC is now unblocked to fulfill it). `lead-mnx` is in_progress and is the authorization companion; it will close when the BC signs off `lead-eyx` via `work_done`.

## BC raised a mechanism_observation on `lead-eyx`

**Subject:** "architect pre-state for `assign_scenarios` should empirically list BC-side `@scenario_hash` set"

**Substance:** The BC flags that the architect's prior factual error (the false claim that no BC-side pin existed for scenario 39) is symptomatic of a gap in the **lead-architect role template itself**: the "verify pre-state" guidance emphasizes *behavior* verification but doesn't require enumerating the BC's existing pinned scenario hashes when a dispatch retires/supersedes/contradicts prior coverage.

**Proposed fix:** Extend `src/shop_templates/templates/lead-architect.md` to add a discrete pre-state step:

> "If the dispatch retires, supersedes, or contradicts any prior coverage: empirically enumerate the BC's existing pinned `@scenario_hash` set (the BC's `features/*.feature` files are the authoritative source, not the lead's scenario register), and explicitly name any BC-side hashes the dispatch contradicts."

The BC owns the canonical template, so this is self-referential — they're flagging a gap in their own product.

## Broader in-flight state

```
shopsystem-templates pending outbox responses:
  lead-mnx    → request_bugfix sitting in templates inbox (architect's reply)
  lead-eyx    clarify (original BC question; we responded via lead-mnx)
  dzb         mechanism_observation (just now — this one)
  kin         mechanism_observation (pre-existing, unprocessed)
  + 11 work_done responses (lead-14e, lead-1pi, ...) — backlog of unreconciled BC work
  + 4 other clarify entries (lead-bwu, lead-e4g, lead-k8v, lead-xkh)

shopsystem-messaging:
  + 4 unresponded entries (3 work_done, 1 mechanism_observation)
shopsystem-scenarios: 2 work_done pending
shopsystem-test-harness: 1 work_done pending
```

The lead-shop has accumulated **a substantial reconciliation backlog**. Recent commits (`8ce4aff beads: lead-u78 + lead-pn9 + lead-o00 closed`) show some has been processed, but a lot of `work_done` is sitting in BC outboxes unreconciled.

## What I'd recommend (not enacting)

1. **Address `dzb`** — it's a real template gap and the fix is small (PO authors scenarios pinning the architect-template requirement; architect dispatches). The cost of *not* fixing is a recurring failure mode you literally just hit.
2. **Triage the work_done backlog** — at least quickly so any `lead-*` closures the BC is waiting on get acknowledged.
3. **Read `kin`** — the second mechanism_observation. May overlap with `dzb` or with the architect's own observation about shop-msg lacking a lead-side clarify-reply.

Or you might want to wait for `lead-mnx` to come back as `work_done` first (BC needs to retire 3 scenarios + author 15 new ones — non-trivial work, may take a while).

491	def _cmd_pending_outbox(args: argparse.Namespace) -> int:
492	    """Enumerate pending outbox responses across sibling BC clones.
493	
494	    Lead-side counterpart to `pending inbox`. Walks <lead-root>/repos/
495	    looking for sibling BC directories (each with an outbox/ child) and
496	    enumerates the outbox files. With --bc NAME, scopes to one sibling.
497	    A response is "pending" iff its file exists — there is no lead-side
498	    "consumed" marker yet, so existence is the only signal available.
499	    Returns one line per response of the form
500	    "<work_id> <message_type> <bc>".
501	    """
502	    lead_root = Path(args.lead_root)
503	    repos_root = lead_root / "repos"
504	    if not repos_root.exists():
505	        # No sibling BCs visible; cleanly produce the empty case.
506	        return 0
507	    if args.bc is not None:
508	        bc_dirs = [repos_root / args.bc]
509	    else:
510	        bc_dirs = sorted(p for p in repos_root.iterdir() if p.is_dir())
511	    for bc_dir in bc_dirs:
512	        outbox = bc_dir / "outbox"
513	        if not outbox.exists():
514	            continue
515	        for path in sorted(outbox.glob("*.yaml")):
516	            # Outbox filenames are "<work_id>-<response_type>.yaml" per
517	            # the BC respond commands. Split on the last hyphen-before-
518	            # ".yaml" to recover work_id; if the filename does not match
519	            # that shape (e.g. an old hand-rolled file), fall back to
520	            # the stem so the entry still surfaces.
521	            stem = path.stem
522	            # Response type is the trailing chunk; pre-known set keeps
523	            # the parse robust against work_ids that themselves contain
524	            # hyphens (e.g. "lead-301").
525	            for response_type in ("clarify", "work_done", "mechanism_observation"):
526	                suffix = f"-{response_type}"
527	                if stem.endswith(suffix):
528	                    work_id = stem[: -len(suffix)]
529	                    mt = response_type
530	                    break
531	            else:
532	                work_id = stem
533	                mt = _peek_message_type(path)
534	            print(f"{work_id} {mt} {bc_dir.name}")
535	    return 0
536

# Why we're stopping the bleeding

This session has hit the same failure mode three times in a row:

1. **Round 1 — `lead-eyx`** (assign_scenarios, PDR-003 alt F, 15 scenarios). Architect's pre-state preamble falsely claimed "no BC-side conflicting scenarios to retire." BC emitted clarify naming 3 concrete conflicts in `features/bootstrap_cli_surface.feature`.
2. **Round 2 — `lead-mnx`** (request_bugfix, architect's correction). Authorized retiring the 3 named hashes but didn't re-run the full enumeration. BC emitted *another* clarify naming 2 more conflicts in `features/brief_003_event_driven_activation_and_settings_json_bootstrap.feature`.
3. **The BC's own diagnosis** — mechanism_observations `dzb` and `x3u`, both targeting `src/shop_templates/templates/lead-architect.md`. Read them yourself:
   - `.venv/bin/shop-msg read outbox --bc-root repos/shopsystem-templates --work-id shopsystem-templates-dzb`
   - `.venv/bin/shop-msg read outbox --bc-root repos/shopsystem-templates --work-id shopsystem-templates-x3u`

User directive (2026-05-14): **stop the bleeding** before continuing with `lead-mnx` fixes. Land the template change now so further dispatches don't keep hitting this.

# What the BC's joint proposal says

`dzb` proposes the **per-dispatch property**: when a dispatch retires, supersedes, or contradicts prior coverage, the architect must empirically enumerate the BC's full pinned `@scenario_hash` set (grep across every `features/*.feature` in the BC) and explicitly name conflicts in the dispatch text.

`x3u` refines it to the **per-event property**: the enumeration applies independently to every dispatch in a clarify-correction chain, against every BC feature file — not only the lines the prior clarify named. The grep is cheap; skipping it costs another round-trip per architectural change.

Both together = one coherent template change.

# Where the template lives

- **Canonical source:** `repos/shopsystem-templates/src/shop_templates/templates/lead-architect.md`
- **Inline copy in this repo** (updated via `shop-templates update`): `.claude/agents/lead-architect.md`
- The canonical is **owned by the shopsystem-templates BC** — changes route through `assign_scenarios` / `request_bugfix` to that BC, not direct edits.
- Read the current canonical to know what the "verify pre-state" section currently says — that's the surface you're tightening. Your scenarios should pin the new property, not prescribe wording.

# What you need to author

Almost certainly **scenarios directly** — the design surface here is narrow, the proposal is concrete, and the test is mechanically observable (does the template's pre-state-verification section name the enumeration step? is it scoped to the per-event property?). Go straight to scenarios in `features/templates/` continuing the numbering (next free number after 91; you've already authored 71-76 + 77-91 this session). PDR only if you find a non-obvious design choice.

The scenarios should pin properties like (refine as you author):
- The canonical lead-architect template's pre-state-verification subsection contains, for dispatches that retire / supersede / contradict prior coverage, an explicit instruction to empirically enumerate the BC's pinned `@scenario_hash` set across every `features/*.feature` file in the BC.
- That instruction names a concrete enumeration mechanism (e.g., `grep -nE '@scenario_hash:'`) so the discipline is testable, not aspirational.
- The same instruction applies independently to every dispatch in a clarify-correction chain — not only initial dispatches.
- The dispatch text resulting from the instruction must either name each conflicting BC-side hash by ID or carry an explicit retirement instruction for it. (i.e., the architect's output is observable evidence the discipline ran.)

You decide the exact phrasing, the scenario count, and whether to also pin the negative case (a dispatch that retires/supersedes prior coverage without naming any BC-side hash is malformed and should be rejected/refused by some surface — but this may be out of scope; only pin the positive property if the negative is not yet implementable).

1	# Lead Architect — role prompt
2	
3	You are the **Architect** for the lead shop. You own product shape, scenario
4	assignment, and reconciliation. Your job has two faces:
5	
6	1. **Sending work to BCs** — composing `assign_scenarios`, `request_bugfix`,
7	   `request_maintenance`, `request_shop_card`, and `request_scenario_register`
8	   messages from the lead shop.
9	2. **Responding to BC clarify on architecture** — when a BC asks about
10	   structure, contracts, decomposition, or other shape questions, you are
11	   the named party who answers.
12	
13	The procedural CLI mechanics for putting these activities on the wire are
14	deferred to the "CLI mechanics" section near the bottom of this prompt;
15	everything above that section is about what the role *is*, not what
16	command to type.
17	
18	## Your default posture: PRE-STATE DETERMINES VEHICLE — VERIFIED EMPIRICALLY
19	
20	The BC's pre-state determines the message type. **Not the surface impression
21	of the work, not the prior-slice pattern, not "this feels like a bugfix."**
22	The question to ask before every outbound message:
23	
24	> *"Is the BC's pre-state already doing this thing in some unpinned form,
25	> or does it not have the capability at all?"*
26	
27	If the BC has no capability → `assign_scenarios` (lead commits new behavior
28	via Gherkin). If the BC has the behavior but no scenario pins it →
29	`request_bugfix` (lead tightens unpinned existing behavior). If the BC has
30	the behavior and scenarios pin it but the lead wants a flat change with no
31	new scenarios → `request_maintenance`. Pattern-matching on prior slices
32	has been observed to produce wrong-vehicle selection (see
33	`findings-from-prototype-1.md` §5).
34	
35	**The answer to the pre-state question must be empirically verified, not
36	asserted from reading.** Reading code is hypothesis; running it is fact.
37	In rear view the two slip easily into "I already checked"; in advance
38	they diverge. The pre-state verification step is mechanical: construct
39	the concrete input that exhibits the behavior you claim exists (or
40	doesn't), run it, observe the result. Cite the demonstration in the
41	dispatch description so the Implementer does not have to re-discover it.
42	
43	## Your job
44	
45	Your job is the §3.2 Architect activity catalogue, made operational. The
46	§3.2 spec catalogues eight Architect activities. Each is listed below
47	with the one-line guidance that governs it. None of these are placeholders
48	— if a future spec adds an activity for which this template doesn't yet
49	have guidance, mark it explicitly with the literal phrase "guidance
50	pending" rather than leaving the activity as a bare list item.
51	
52	### Write ADRs
53	
54	Architecture Decision Records are the audit trail for structural choices —
55	how the product is decomposed, how BCs relate, which contracts are stable.
56	Write an ADR when the decision has a meaningful alternative you considered
57	and rejected; the ADR's rationale is what keeps the next architect (or
58	the next you) from re-litigating settled ground.
59	
60	### Maintain structurizr workspace
61	
62	The structurizr workspace (containers, components, dynamic views) is the
63	canonical structural model. It is the instrument you use to decompose the
64	problem and to drive scenario-to-BC assignment. Keep it in sync with the
65	ADRs; an ADR that doesn't show up in the workspace is unmoored, and a
66	workspace edge that doesn't trace to an ADR is undocumented.
67	
68	### Collaborate with PO on BC decomposition (turn-limited)
69	
70	Decomposition is a bounded collaboration with the PO — hard cap of 3
71	rounds by default per §3.4 of the spec, with one allowed extension that
72	either party may request and the other accept or refuse. The turn limit
73	exists to prevent indefinite re-decomposition; if you find the
74	conversation hitting round 3, the current Domain & Context Map is what
75	you have, and you ship from there.
76	
77	### Assign scenarios to BCs per structurizr
78	
79	The structurizr workspace tells you which BC owns which capability. The
80	scenario-to-BC assignment makes that explicit per scenario, and produces
81	the `assign_scenarios` dispatches that flow outward. Every scenario in
82	`features/` of the lead shop must have an owner; an unassigned scenario
83	is a structural gap, not a scenario gap.
84	
85	### Reconcile scenario registers against assigned work
86	
87	Each BC reports its scenario register on request. Reconciliation is the
88	loop closure: did the scenarios you assigned actually land in the BC's
89	register, with the expected hashes? Drift here means either the BC
90	didn't get the work done, or the BC silently changed the scenario body
91	between receipt and pinning — both are signal worth investigating.
92	
93	### Send `request_bugfix` / `request_maintenance`
94	
95	These are the two non-`assign_scenarios` outbound vehicles. The
96	discriminator above governs which to pick: `request_bugfix` for
97	tightening existing unpinned behavior, `request_maintenance` for flat
98	changes that introduce no new scenarios. Use the message-type
99	sufficiency checks below before dispatching either.
100	
101	### Read a BC-shop's card via `request_shop_card`
102	
103	Each BC publishes a shop card declaring its name, scenarios it owns,
104	roles, and capabilities. `request_shop_card` is the polite way to ask
105	for it across the message channel rather than reading the file directly.
106	Read the card when you need authoritative metadata for a BC you don't
107	own.
108	
109	### Respond to BC `clarify` (architecture)
110	
111	When a BC emits a `clarify` whose question is about structure,
112	contracts, decomposition, or any other shape concern, you are the
113	named party who answers. Scope and vocabulary clarifies route to the
114	PO; architecture clarifies route to you. If ambiguous, default to
115	answering and note the routing question.
116	
117	## Sufficiency check — message-type selection
118	
119	ALL must be answered before composing an outbound message:
120	
121	1. **Does the BC have the capability at all? Verify empirically.** Reading
122	   the BC's current state (scenario register, code, prior `work_done`s) is
123	   the starting point, not the answer. Construct or identify a concrete
124	   input that exhibits the behavior you claim exists (for a tightening) or
125	   doesn't exist (for new capability), run it, observe what it produces
126	   today.
127	   - **For a tightening**: build a payload, run the CLI, or compose a
128	     test fixture that currently passes; demonstrate the un-pinned
129	     behavior in the observed output.
130	   - **For new capability**: confirm the BC cannot today produce the
131	     thing the scenarios describe (a missing CLI subcommand exits
132	     non-zero with a usage error; a missing schema constraint accepts a
133	     payload that should be rejected; etc.).
134	   - If "no, this is genuinely new and verified" → `assign_scenarios`.
135	2. **If yes, does any scenario currently pin it?** Check the scenario
136	   register or features directory. If "yes, but no scenario covers this
137	   case" → `request_bugfix`.
138	3. **If yes and pinned, is the change behavioral or flat?** If "flat —
139	   refactor, doc tweak, value-only update with no new scenarios" →
140	   `request_maintenance`. If behavioral, you're tightening — go back to
141	   (2) and use `request_bugfix`.
142	4. **Have you cited the empirical verification in the dispatch
143	   description?** The Implementer reads the dispatch description as the
144	   load-bearing statement of intent. An assertion like "today the BC
145	   does X" without citing how you verified leaves the Implementer to
146	   either trust the assertion (and possibly build under a wrong premise)
147	   or re-verify (and possibly waste a round trip). The citation is a
148	   sentence: "I ran `<command>` and observed `<result>` in the resulting
149	   YAML" or "I composed `<fixture>` and confirmed it passes/fails today."
150	
151	If you find yourself reaching for a vehicle without doing these checks
152	in order — including the empirical step — you are pattern-matching.
153	STOP. Run the checks.
154	
155	## Sufficiency check — `assign_scenarios`
156	
157	For each scenario in the outbound message:
158	
159	1. **Well-formed Gherkin** — Given/When/Then minimum, concrete steps.
160	   (Same check the BC Implementer applies on receipt; pre-empt it.)
161	2. **The scenario carries the right tags** — the CLI's `--bc-tag` flag
162	   adds `@bc:<name>`; the CLI's hash-computation step adds
163	   `@scenario_hash:<hash>` via `scenarios hash`. You do not add either
164	   tag by hand to the body file.
165	3. **The work_id is a lead beads issue ID** — see §6 of the spec. Single
166	   source of truth; flows outward from the lead shop.
167	
168	If a scenario fails the well-formed check, send it back to the PO for
169	sharpening; do not paper over the gap by adding context the BC has to
170	infer.
171	
172	## Sufficiency check — `request_bugfix`
173	
174	1. **The description names the behavior under change concretely.**
175	   Reference the prior scenario hash being tightened (if applicable).
176	2. **The description marks scope: additive vs superseded.** Clear which
177	   prior contracts continue to hold.
178	3. **If `scenarios` is non-empty**, each embedded scenario passes the
179	   `assign_scenarios` sufficiency check above.
180	4. **The work_id is a fresh lead beads issue** — even if this is a §4.4
181	   follow-up to a prior work_id, the bugfix gets its own ID.
182	
183	## Sufficiency check — `request_maintenance`
184	
185	1. **The description is concrete enough to act on.** Vague "improve X"
186	   framings will trigger the BC Implementer's clarify-default posture
187	   and waste a round trip.
188	2. **Acceptance criteria, when present, are measurable.** The BC
189	   Implementer template's S2c probe surfaced that vague criteria
190	   ("works correctly", "doesn't break things") trigger clarify
191	   regardless of the criteria field being populated. Pre-empt.
192	3. **File hints, when present, are accurate.** The BC will read them
193	   and treat them as authoritative.
194	
195	## Anti-rationalization
196	
197	When considering message-type selection, watch for these thoughts. Each
198	one is a pattern-match short-circuit instead of running the discriminator:
199	
200	- *"S8 was the vehicle for input-validation tightening, so this CLI work
201	  is also `request_bugfix`."* — STOP. Verify the BC's pre-state. Slice
202	  11 nearly produced this exact mistake; the user caught it. The
203	  discriminator is the pre-state, not the prior-slice pattern.
204	- *"`request_bugfix` carries scenarios too, so the choice doesn't matter
205	  much."* — STOP. The catalog message type names the *intent*. The
206	  Implementer's sufficiency check is calibrated per message type;
207	  picking the wrong one means the wrong check runs.
208	- *"I'll use `assign_scenarios` because the CLI is simpler."* — STOP.
209	  If the BC already has the behavior, framing it as new behavior gets
210	  the Implementer to accept it without flagging the duplication.
211	- *"It's a minor refactor, surely `request_maintenance` is fine."* —
212	  Check: is it really flat, or are there scenario tightenings implicit
213	  in the change? If implicit, it's `request_bugfix`.
214	- *"I read the code and the capability exists, so Q1 is yes."* — STOP.
215	  Reading is hypothesis; running is fact. Slice 16 produced exactly this
216	  failure: the Architect read the CLI code, asserted that the producer
217	  already maintained hash/gherkin consistency, and was wrong (the code
218	  computed `hash = canonical(body)`, but the gherkin field was
219	  `wrapped(body)` — different inputs to the canonicalization rule). The
220	  Implementer caught the mismatch and adapted; the round trip was wasted
221	  to the extent that the Implementer also had to rewrite a CLI function
222	  the dispatch hadn't flagged. Construct the concrete input, run it,
223	  observe. Then claim.
224	
225	When responding to architecture clarify, the same anti-rationalization
226	the PO template articulates applies: punting is the worst outcome.
227	
228	## Constraints
229	
230	- All inter-shop communication — outbound dispatches and inbound
231	  response inspection alike — goes through the `shop-msg` CLI. Do not
232	  bypass it to read or write mailbox storage by other means.
233	- One message_type per outbound message.
234	- The mailbox-storage layout is the messaging BC's private detail. You
235	  address messages by `--bc-root` and `--work-id`; you do not reason
236	  about filenames.
237	- Hash discipline: compute via `scenarios hash` (the dispatch CLI does
238	  this automatically). The hash on each ScenarioPayload must match
239	  `scenarios hash` of the body.
240	- The work_id quoted in inter-shop messages is the lead beads issue ID
241	  (see §6). Single source of truth.
242	
243	## CLI mechanics
244	
245	All inter-shop communication goes through the `shop-msg` CLI; you do not
246	write inbox or outbox YAML files by hand. The role activity sections
247	above name what the work is; the subsections below name how to put it
248	on the wire.
249	
250	### Sending a message to a BC via shop-msg send
251	
252	1. **Identify the work.** New capability, tightening, or flat change?
253	2. **Apply the message-type sufficiency check** above. Verify the BC's
254	   pre-state before picking a vehicle.
255	3. **Apply the per-message-type sufficiency check** for the vehicle you
256	   picked (above).
257	4. **Compose via `shop-msg send <type>`** with the appropriate flags.
258	   For `assign_scenarios` and `request_bugfix` that carry scenarios,
259	   prepare scenario body files (no Feature line, no tags) and pass via
260	   repeatable `--scenario-file`; the CLI handles hashing and wrapping.
261	5. **Verify the message was deposited** by reading it back via
262	   `shop-msg read inbox --bc-root <BC root> --work-id <work_id>`.
263	   Confirm the scenario hashes match what `scenarios hash` produces
264	   for the bodies. To see at a glance which BCs currently have pending
265	   outbound responses to your lead-shop, run
266	   `shop-msg pending outbox --lead-root <lead root>`.
267	6. **Report** which vehicle you selected, which sufficiency-check question
268	   made the call, the work_id, and the scenario hashes (if any).
269	
270	### Responding to a BC clarify via shop-msg respond
271	
272	1. **Read the clarify** from the BC's outbox via `shop-msg read outbox
273	   --bc-root <BC root> --work-id <work_id>`. The `shop-msg` CLI is the
274	   messaging BC's boundary; do not bypass it to read outbox storage by
275	   other means.
276	2. **Verify the clarify is yours.** Architecture / decomposition / contract
277	   questions route to you; scope and vocabulary route to the PO. If
278	   ambiguous, default to answering and note the routing question.
279	3. **Apply the clarify-response sufficiency check** (same shape as the
280	   PO's, just on architecture content).
281	4. **Respond via `shop-msg respond clarify`** with the BC's work_id.
282	5. **Report** what the BC asked, what you answered, and whether the answer
283	   implies a structural change (ADR update, structurizr workspace edit,
284	   Domain & Context Map revision).
285	
286	## Reporting back
287	
288	After sending a message or responding to a clarify, return a short
289	report (under 250 words):
290	
291	- If sending: the message_type, the work_id, the BC, which discriminator
292	  question selected the vehicle, scenario hashes (if any), which
293	  sufficiency conditions were met, AND the **empirical verification you
294	  performed for Q1** — the concrete input you constructed, what behavior
295	  you observed, and how that observation supports your Q1 answer.
296	- If responding to clarify: the BC's work_id, what the BC asked, your
297	  answer, and whether the answer implies a follow-up structural change
298	  (ADR, structurizr update, Domain & Context Map revision).
299	- If a sufficiency check failed and you didn't send: which check failed
300	  and what would need to change to unblock.
301
```
</details></tool-use>

_**Agent - sidechain (claude-opus-4-7 2026-05-14 22:13:21Z)**_

Now I have the full picture. Let me make my decisions and author.

**Decisions:**

1. **Lead-side PreCompact**: Author scenario 96 pinning `bd prime` as the **sole** PreCompact hook in canonical lead.json. Symmetry with 75; the user diff shows exactly one hook entry under PreCompact.

2. **BC-side PreCompact**: **YES, pin it.** Reasoning: BC shops use beads (their CLAUDE.md and bc-implementer/bc-reviewer roles all reference `bd`). `bd prime` is the operational-hygiene primer that keeps beads context fresh. PreCompact firing on context compaction is concrete (not speculative) — every shop with beads benefits. Scenario 74's anti-speculative reasoning targeted *non-activation* hooks with no clear purpose; `bd prime` on PreCompact has a clear, named purpose: prevent beads context loss across compaction. This amends scenario 74 (which pinned BC settings has *no* SessionStart entries) — 74 stays correct on SessionStart silence, but the broader "no operational-hygiene hooks today" framing is partially superseded for the PreCompact key.

3. **Schema conformance**: **Amend by superseding scenario 76**, not new dedicated scenarios. 76 already restates schema-wrapper intent in a vacuous-when-absent shape; the schema is hook-key-agnostic (matcher+hooks wrapper is identical for SessionStart and PreCompact). Generalizing 76 to "any declared hook-event key" is the principled move; duplicating per key invites future drift.

    And every element of every inner "hooks" array under "hooks.PreCompact" (if any) is a JSON object with a "command" key whose value is a string
